summary: Total lung capacity equals vital capacity plus residual volume.
Total lung capacity equals tidal volume plus residual volume.
Total lung capacity equals functional residual capacity plus tidal volume.
Oxygen is transported mainly bound to haemoglobin (98.5%) with only 1.5% dissolved in plasma.
Oxygen is transported mainly dissolved in plasma (98.5%) with 1.5% bound to haemoglobin.
Oxygen and carbon dioxide are transported equally by haemoglobin.
The oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve shifts right with increased temperature, increased CO2, decreased pH, and increased 2,3-DPG.
The curve shifts right with decreased temperature and increased pH.
The curve shifts left with increased temperature and decreased pH.
Carbon dioxide is transported in blood as bicarbonate (70%), carbaminohaemoglobin (23%), and dissolved in plasma (7%).
Carbon dioxide is transported mainly dissolved in plasma (70%).
Carbon dioxide is transported exclusively as carbaminohaemoglobin.
The medullary respiratory centre is the primary controller of ventilation, with peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid body responding to low PaO2.
The pontine respiratory centre is the primary controller of ventilation.
Peripheral chemoreceptors respond primarily to high PaCO2 rather than low PaO2.
Surfactant produced by type II pneumocytes reduces surface tension and prevents alveolar collapse.
Surfactant is produced by type I pneumocytes and increases surface tension.
Surfactant production begins in the first trimester of pregnancy.
Fetal haemoglobin has a higher affinity for oxygen than adult haemoglobin, facilitating oxygen transfer across the placenta.
Fetal haemoglobin has a lower affinity for oxygen than adult haemoglobin.
Fetal and adult haemoglobin have identical oxygen affinities.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise gas transport and lung volumes.
